caveats: a: OEWS combines NAICS 313310 and 313320 and therefore is not a six-digit occupation mix.
a: The inspection system source is demonstrated on woven-fabric production rather than coated fabric and does not measure labor savings.
a: Exposure denotes substitutable tasks or avoided hiring, not whole-job removal.
rho: The MLC sample is broad manufacturing and may skew toward digitally mature or larger respondents.
rho: Vision systems include conventional automation as well as AI and may not be deployed on coating lines.
rho: Customer and environmental validation can prevent technically feasible process changes from being implemented.
e: No public dataset measures lens eligibility for NAICS 313320 in the specified EBITDA band.
e: The provided LMM firm count is an estimate and may include facilities whose margins are temporarily in range.
e: Environmental liabilities can impair transferability independently of operating performance.
s5: The succession evidence covers employer businesses across industries and sizes.
s5: Stated plans may not be completed and can include transfers that do not convey control.
s5: Undiscovered environmental liabilities can turn a planned transfer into a closure or asset sale.
q: No public evidence measures five-year retained AI benefit for fabric coaters.
q: Defense and specialty programs may retain materially more than commodity coated fabrics.
q: The estimate excludes demand loss and implementation failure to avoid double counting.
d5: The industrial-production series combines finishing and coating.
d5: Specialty and defense growth may not benefit the current customer baskets of all screened firms.
d5: Chemical restrictions can destroy legacy product demand even while creating demand for reformulated coatings.
o: This estimate is conditional on year-five demand and does not reapply the d5 decline.
o: Some formulation, inspection, and documentation tasks can move to customers even though physical coating cannot.
o: A chemistry ban can eliminate a current coating specification rather than merely change its operator.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Per-yard, per-roll, per-pound, per-lot, or program pricing for toll coating and conversion, plus product margins where mills purchase substrate or chemistry. Customers span industrial, automotive, defense, protective, outdoor, furnishings, and other technical-textile markets; approvals, performance specifications, recipes, and qualification histories support repeat orders but seldom subscription contracts.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code includes both toll coaters serving external customers and own-account coated-fabric manufacturers. The lens is narrowed to repeat external-customer coating programs so that outsourced-service and product-manufacturing economics are not combined.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive coating lines inside integrated manufacturers, own-account commodity product operations without an outsourced-service relationship, textile dyeing and finishing mills, paper or non-textile coating, nonrecurring experimental work without repeat revenue, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market mills repeatedly coating, laminating, varnishing, waxing, or rubberizing textile fabrics or apparel for external B2B customers under toll-coating, approved-supplier, or recurring production programs.

a.bridge: BLS measures the workforce of NAICS 313300, combining fabric finishing and coating, rather than the wage-weighted AI exposure of NAICS 313320. The estimate maps office support, planning, quoting, specification review, inspection, traceability, process analysis, and selected supervisory tasks to partial exposure while treating coating-line setup, chemical handling, maintenance, material movement, and physical intervention as largely unexposed. The broader-group mix may overstate dyeing work and understate coating-machine work, creating error in either direction.
caveats: OEWS combines NAICS 313310 and 313320 and therefore is not a six-digit occupation mix.
The inspection system source is demonstrated on woven-fabric production rather than coated fabric and does not measure labor savings.
Exposure denotes substitutable tasks or avoided hiring, not whole-job removal.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from 10-15 independent US coaters, segmented across formulation, lab trials, line setup and tending, visual and functional testing, maintenance, planning, quoting, compliance, traceability, sales, and administration, would materially revise the range.
a.rationale: The broader group reports 12.81% of employment in office and administrative support, 4.11% in sales, and meaningful management, engineering, science, and inspection work. Machine vision can automate portions of fabric inspection, while AI can assist formulation search, specification parsing, anomaly detection, scheduling, quoting, documentation, and predictive maintenance. The core remains physical process manufacturing, including coating-machine operation, setup, materials, curing, testing, and maintenance.

d5.bridge: Federal Reserve industrial production measures real output for NAICS 3133, combining finishing and coating, not constant-quality demand for the current NAICS 313320 repeat-service basket. The index declined from 66.3922 in 2022 to 60.3558 in 2025, supporting downside. The base assumes moderation rather than full trend extrapolation, while the upper case reflects specialty and industrial applications and domestic-source defense programs. Finishing may account for an unknown share of the observed contraction, so the broad proxy can misstate coating demand in either direction.
caveats: The industrial-production series combines finishing and coating.
Specialty and defense growth may not benefit the current customer baskets of all screened firms.
Chemical restrictions can destroy legacy product demand even while creating demand for reformulated coatings.
d5.change_evidence: NAICS 313320 real shipment volume by chemistry and end market, backlog and utilization from independent coaters, HTS-level import penetration for comparable coated fabrics, and five-year sourcing plans from automotive, defense, industrial, outdoor, and protective customers would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Broad finishing-and-coating output has contracted, and commodity coated fabric remains exposed to imports and downstream cyclicality. Coated technical textiles nevertheless serve diverse automotive, industrial, protective, outdoor, furnishings, composite, and defense uses. Domestic-source restrictions explicitly cover coated synthetic fabric for relevant Defense Department acquisition, while specification and qualification requirements can anchor incumbent programs.

caveats: No public dataset measures lens eligibility for NAICS 313320 in the specified EBITDA band.
The provided LMM firm count is an estimate and may include facilities whose margins are temporarily in range.
Environmental liabilities can impair transferability independently of operating performance.
e.change_evidence: A census of the 55 estimated LMM-band firms showing toll versus own-account revenue, repeat-order share, customer concentration, approval status, equipment and site ownership, permits, coating chemistries, PFAS history, and stand-alone management would directly determine eligibility.
e.rationale: Coated-fabric customers frequently need repeat runs to qualified specifications, and independent mills can transfer recipes, equipment, permits, customer approvals, and process know-how. Eligibility is reduced because NAICS classification does not distinguish toll coating from own-account product manufacture, and because captive facilities, single-customer dependencies, distressed equipment, real-estate issues, or chemical and PFAS liabilities may make an apparently operating firm unsuitable or nontransferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 313320 ($470,450,250) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 313320 ($2,795,851,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $395,062 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 313320); class <5: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $713,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $108,773 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,758,941 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $268,239 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,403,056 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $671,466 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,103,444 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,998,275 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $68,110,870 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,386,908 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 10 firms, avg revenue/firm $50,909,100 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,763,638 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 55

caveats: This estimate is conditional on year-five demand and does not reapply the d5 decline.
Some formulation, inspection, and documentation tasks can move to customers even though physical coating cannot.
A chemistry ban can eliminate a current coating specification rather than merely change its operator.
o.change_evidence: Customer sourcing studies separating third-party US toll coating from vertical integration, imported pre-coated materials, alternative substrates, and software-enabled process self-service would test the remaining operator-required share.
o.rationale: Coating, laminating, varnishing, waxing, and rubberizing transform physical textiles through material preparation, application, curing, testing, handling, and accountable chemical and environmental control. Software can assist formulation, inspection, records, scheduling, and line control but cannot deliver coated fabric without a physical operator. Residual displacement comes from customer vertical integration, imported pre-coated fabric, material substitution, or qualification of different production routes rather than software alone.

caveats: No public evidence measures five-year retained AI benefit for fabric coaters.
Defense and specialty programs may retain materially more than commodity coated fabrics.
The estimate excludes demand loss and implementation failure to avoid double counting.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and quote histories showing open-book provisions, surcharge mechanics, rebid cadence, qualification costs, warranty or rework allocation, customer concentration, and margins before and after automation would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Coating programs are commonly priced per physical unit rather than billed as transparent labor hours, so lower scrap, fewer defects, improved line uptime, faster documentation, and avoided overhead hiring can initially accrue to the operator. Qualified recipes, performance testing, customer approvals, technical support, low-volume complexity, and domestic sourcing requirements can slow competitive pass-through. Retention is lower in commodity toll work, open-book or cost-plus contracts, frequent rebids, and segments where rivals can adopt the same tools.

rho.bridge: The Manufacturing Leadership Council survey measures broad manufacturers' AI technology use, strategy, investment, and barriers, not the fraction of exposed labor opportunity implemented by LMM fabric coaters within five years. Its high reported vision-system use indicates technical readiness, but only 18% report a formal AI strategy for operations and majorities report inadequate data, skills, ROI evidence, management trust, and legacy integration. Translating this evidence to small coating mills likely overstates resources and current data maturity, so the estimate discounts broad adoption materially.
caveats: The MLC sample is broad manufacturing and may skew toward digitally mature or larger respondents.
Vision systems include conventional automation as well as AI and may not be deployed on coating lines.
Customer and environmental validation can prevent technically feasible process changes from being implemented.
rho.change_evidence: Installation and operating data from US toll coaters and their coating-line, inspection, MES, lab, and predictive-maintenance vendors—showing deployment, false rejects, recipe changes, qualification time, realized hours saved, and payback—would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Inspection, document, planning, and customer-service tools are implementable sooner than closed-loop coating control. The survey reports 72% current vision-system use among respondents and increased AI investment intent, but also reports that 65% lack the right data, 82% lack skills, and 78% struggle with legacy systems or integration. Coaters also face recipe variation, safety, emissions, performance testing, customer requalification, and chemical substitutions that make uncontrolled automation costly.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public their business within five years, and that 52.3% of employer businesses were owned by people age 55 or older. These are plans across industries, not completed control transfers of LMM fabric coaters. The estimate removes unconsummated plans, non-control changes, closures, and nontransferable operations and applies a further downward adjustment for capital intensity and environmental diligence; it allows genuine family, management, and third-party control transfers.
caveats: The succession evidence covers employer businesses across industries and sizes.
Stated plans may not be completed and can include transfers that do not convey control.
Undiscovered environmental liabilities can turn a planned transfer into a closure or asset sale.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year record of closed control sales, family or management transfers, closures, owner ages, sale mandates, and withdrawn processes for all independent US fabric coaters would support an industry-specific hazard rate.
s5.rationale: The aging employer-owner population creates a real transition pipeline, but hazardous-chemical history, PFAS exposure, emissions and wastewater obligations, site ownership, specialized equipment, qualification transfer, and customer concentration can delay or prevent a coating-mill sale. The small estimated population also makes observed deal flow lumpy.

principal_driver: Qualified repeat coating programs can retain value from better inspection, process knowledge, uptime, traceability, and overhead automation.
principal_weakness: Legacy chemical and environmental liabilities can make an otherwise attractive operating business difficult or impossible to transfer.

caveats: The current FRED series has revised its 2025 value to 60.0771 from the packet's 60.3558; the broader contraction remains supported, and the correction does not change the base tier or action.

The eligibility base e=0.75 is an honest ESTIMATE, but S1 and S5 establish industry scope and environmental risk rather than the share of LMM firms that fit the narrowed repeat-program lens; the proposed firm census remains necessary.

The retention base q=0.60 is an honest ESTIMATE with no direct public five-year retention evidence; S7 and S8 support demand context and end-market breadth, not pricing pass-through or retained AI benefit.

The a and rho evidence relies on a combined finishing-and-coating workforce, a weaving-machine inspection example, and a broad manufacturing AI survey rather than LMM fabric-coater task and deployment data; these proxy limitations are disclosed and the ranges span the resulting uncertainty.

sources_audited.note: The Census definition supports the physical coating, laminating, varnishing, waxing, and rubberizing scope and the exclusion of textile finishing, but it does not establish repeat external-customer programs, transferability, or lens eligibility; those portions of e and o remain disclosed estimates.

The BLS page is explicitly for NAICS 313300 and reports the cited occupation shares, including office support, sales, production, inspectors, and coating-machine operators. The broader-industry limitation is correctly disclosed.

The PolyU page reports real-time full-width fabric inspection, around 40 defect types, and a detection rate above 90%. It demonstrates weaving inspection rather than coated-fabric labor savings, a limitation already disclosed.

The survey reports 72% current vision-system use, 18% with an operations AI strategy, and the cited data, skills, ROI/trust, and legacy-integration barriers. It is broad manufacturing evidence rather than a fabric-coater adoption measure, as the proxy bridge states.

The EPA report covers textile mills, documents PFAS performance uses and coating application, notes sparse monitoring, and reports PFAS detections in wastewater from a small number of textile mills. It supports the environmental-risk direction but not an industry-specific transferability rate.

Gallup reports the cited 52.3% owner-age statistic and 22% five-year sale/transfer plan among employer-business owners, with survey methods disclosed. The cross-industry plans-to-completions bridge is appropriately labeled PROXY.

FRED supports the NAICS 3133 real-output construct and the contraction direction, but the live revised series now displays 60.0771 for 2025 rather than the packet's 60.3558. This small revision does not alter the demand conclusion, base tier, or action.

SelectUSA supports the cited $25.3 billion textile-mill shipment statistic and broad specialty and industrial end markets, including automotive, protective, filtration, insulation, hoses, tires, and composites. It does not directly measure fabric-coater demand or five-year retained AI benefit.
The Trade.gov page states the Berry Amendment domestic-source restriction for synthetic and coated synthetic fabric and the other cited textile categories, subject to listed exceptions.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily engaged in coating, laminating, varnishing, waxing, and rubberizing textiles and apparel.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 313320 Fabric Coating Mills

sources.supports: Reports the broader group's occupation mix, including office support at 12.81%, sales at 4.11%, production at 56.59%, inspectors at 5.14%, and coating-machine operators at 6.16%.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Textile and Fabric Finishing and Fabric Coating Mills

sources.supports: Describes real-time AI machine-vision inspection across fabric width, detecting around 40 defect types at a reported detection rate above 90%.
sources.title: WiseEye: AI-based Textile Material Inspection System

sources.supports: Reports broad manufacturer use of vision systems and machine learning, increased investment intent, but only 18% with a formal operations AI strategy and major data, skills, ROI, trust, and legacy-system barriers.
sources.title: Shaping the AI-Powered Factory of the Future: Survey Report

sources.supports: Documents fabric coating and laminating within regulated textile-mill processes, PFAS use to impart performance properties, coating application routes, sparse monitoring, and observed PFAS in some textile-mill wastewater discharges.
sources.title: Multi-Industry PFAS Study: 2021 Preliminary Report

sources.supports: Reports that 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older and that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; provides survey methods.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Federal Reserve real-output index for the broader group: 66.3922 in 2022, 61.8800 in 2024, and 60.3558 in 2025.
sources.title: Industrial Production: Textile and Fabric Finishing and Fabric Coating Mills (NAICS 3133)

sources.supports: Describes growth and broad applications in specialty and industrial fabrics, including automotive, protective, filtration, insulation, hoses, tires, and composites, and reports textile-mill shipments of $25.3 billion in 2023.
sources.title: SelectUSA Textiles Industry

sources.supports: States Defense Department domestic-source restrictions covering synthetic fabric and coated synthetic fabric, as well as tents, tarpaulins, covers, canvas products, and specified clothing materials.
sources.title: Berry Amendment Implementation

title: Fabric Coating Mills
